topic: A person finds out their article was published under someone else's name
raw_output:
[story 1]

The notification popped up on the screen, a jarring intrusion into the otherwise quiet afternoon. A chill, entirely unrelated to the lukewarm coffee in my hand, snaked down my spine. I clicked it open, my fingers moving with a practiced slowness, as if the task ahead required monumental effort. The headline screamed my name, but the byline belonged to some stranger. A wave of profound apathy washed over me. Why bother tracking this down? The effort required to correct this error seemed disproportionate to the actual outcome. I leaned back in my chair, the springs groaning a familiar complaint. The whole situation felt like an unnecessary hurdle, a complication I’d rather ignore. I minimized the window, the urge to just leave the browser open and scroll to something more engaging almost overwhelming.

[story 2]

I stared at the screen, the words swimming before my eyes. It was undeniable: the piece I’d painstakingly crafted, the one that had taken weeks of focused, albeit uninspired, effort, was credited to someone else. A dull thud resonated in my chest, a sense of heavy inertia settling over my limbs. My eyelids felt weighted, the simple act of reading the accompanying text demanding a level of engagement I simply didn't possess right now. I let my gaze drift to the dusty bookshelf behind my desk, tracing the spines with a languid finger. Fixing this felt like climbing a mountain when a comfortable hammock was just a few feet away. I sighed, a long, drawn-out sound that seemed to deflate some of the energy I had left. Tomorrow, perhaps.

[story 3]

The email arrived, stark and accusatory. My stomach tightened with a familiar, low-grade resistance. I opened the attachment, and there it was—my work, hijacked. A flicker of annoyance crossed my features, quickly smoothed over by a deep-seated desire to simply put the device down. My shoulders slumped, a deliberate act of yielding to the weight of the discovery. There was no immediate surge of frantic energy; instead, a profound stillness descended. I picked up the remote control, idly turning the television volume up, seeking a distraction so potent it could momentarily erase the unpleasantness of the situation. The thought of diving into the tedious process of reclamation seemed exhausting, an unnecessary expenditure of reserves I was currently unwilling to deploy.
